title: Using a Diffusion Model for Pedestrian Trajectory Prediction in Semi-Open Autonomous Driving Environments
text_excerpt:
[page 1]
17208 IEEE SENSORS JOURNAL, VOL. 24, NO. 10, 15 MAY 2024
Using a Diffusion Model for Pedestrian
Trajectory Prediction in Semi-Open
Autonomous Driving Environments
Yingjuan Tang
 , Hongwen He
 , Senior Member, IEEE, Y ong Wang
 , and Yifan Wu
Abstract—In recent years, the pervasive deployment and
progression of autonomous driving technology have engen-
dered heightened demands, particularly within the intricate
campus and surrounding environments frequently traversed
by autonomous delivery vehicles, such as automated food
delivery and courier services. Accurately predicting pedes-
trian trajectories is paramount in the realm of autonomous
driving. In the face of complex scenarios within campus
and surrounding environments, traditional pedestrian trajec-
tory prediction methods have failed to achieve satisfactory
results. To address this challenge systematically, this arti-
cle uses a digital twin methodology to establish a novel
dataset, denoted as the vulnerable pedestrian trajectory pre-
diction dataset (VPT), grounded in the authentic road network
structures of six campuses and their environs. This article
proposed a universal transformer diffusion modeling for vul-
nerable pedestrian trajectory prediction (UTD-PTP) trajectory
prediction framework based on the diffusion model, which
seeks to forecast pedestrian trajectories in settings char-
acterized by heightened pedestrian traffic, disorderliness,
and irregularities. Importantly, the applicability of our proposed methodology extends beyond campus environments,
showcasing commendable performance on standard autonomous driving datasets. Experimental results reveal an
average enhancement of 0.03 in ADE and 0.05 in FDE on publicly available datasets. On the VPT dataset, our method
demonstrates substantial improvements of 0.12 in ADE and 0.38 in FDE relative to the baseline model. Overall, our
proposed method exhibits superiority in pedestrian trajectory prediction models, substantially reinforcing confidence in
the safety of vulnerable road users in autonomous driving.
Index Terms— Autonomous driving, data-driven model, diffusion model, pedestrian trajectory prediction, vulnerable
traffic participants.
I. I NTRODUCTION
I

driving technology has ushered in significant transforma-
tions in the landscape of urban transportation. Nevertheless,
notwithstanding the strides in technological advancement that

have introduced novel prospects for transportation systems,
ensuring the safety and effective interaction of vulnerable
road users (encompassing pedestrians, cyclists, and electric
cyclists) remains a pivotal challenge essential for the holistic
success of intelligent transportation systems [1], [2]. Particu-
larly within intricate urban environments, the elevated levels of
uncertainty and dynamic variability characterizing pedestrians,
alongside other vulnerable road users, present formidable
challenges during the decision-making and planning phases of
autonomous driving [3]. In contrast to the dynamic trajectories
of vehicles and cyclists, the motion patterns of pedestrians
exhibit heightened levels of uncertainty and randomness, with
their movement directions and velocities being particularly
susceptible to frequent alterations. Research indicates that
effectively predicting the motion trajectories of pedestrians
within a 30-cm range is instrumental in avoiding collisions
with vehicles [4]. Further analysis reveals that implementing

emergency braking by vehicles 0.16 s in advance can lead
to a 50% reduction in casualty rates in traffic accidents [5].
Therefore, the investigation of pedestrian trajectory predic-
tion is essential within the domain of autonomous driving,
as it holds the promise of elevating safety and optimizing
overall traffic efficiency throughout the autonomous driving
process.
The task of predicting pedestrian trajectories involves fore-
casting the future trajectory coordinates of a known historical
trajectory. Previous studies initially tackled the pedestrian
trajectory prediction task using kinematic models, specifi-
cally including the constant velocity (CV) model [6] and
constant acceleration (CA) model [7]. Models of this kind
use fundamental models such as recursive Bayesian filters
to formulate pedestrian states, demonstrating relatively good
performance and low computational costs. Recently, the con-
cept of stochastic trajectory prediction has been introduced
to capture the possibilities of future pedestrian trajectories,
aiming to accurately model the distribution of future trajec-
tories. Currently, methods based on deep learning generative
models have achieved significant success in this field. Some
approaches leverage generative adversarial networks (GANs)
[8] to model the future distribution of trajectories, while others
integrate conditional variational autoencoders (CV AEs) [9] to
provide more stable model training solutions, thereby better
adapting to the target distribution.
While current research has made notable progress in
addressing the challenge of predicting pedestrian trajectories,
there exists a comparative limitation. The dynamic evolution
of uncertainty in pedestrian trajectories is not adequately
modeled. The trajectory motion of pedestrians is characterized
by a notable degree of uncertainty, primarily stemming from
the inherent unpredictability of human behavior [10]. Explicit
representation is typically based on physical principles and
kinematic laws [11], which can more accurately represent
the interaction between agents [6]. However, due to the
limited depth of this representation, the prediction model
is not comprehensive [12]. Implicit representations are not
directly observable and require inference or learning. These
features may encompass the pedestrian’s intentions, behavioral
patterns, habitual movements, and so forth [13]. Implicit
features offer a more comprehensive context and compre-
hension, thereby enhancing the accuracy and robustness of
trajectory prediction [14]. In contrast to prior methodologies
using explicit representations for extracting trajectory features,
the objective of this article is to leverage implicit repre-
sentations for modeling the diffusion of motion uncertainty,
thereby shaping stochastic trajectory predictions. In addition,
in the domain of pedestrian trajectory prediction, construct-
ing effective datasets and proposing corresponding solutions
represent the primary steps in addressing challenges. Ini-
tially, we investigated the application of pedestrian trajectory
prediction methods in campus and surrounding scenarios.
The continuous evolution of autonomous driving technology
has introduced new demands, such as the navigation of
autonomous delivery vehicles and courier vehicles in intricate
and congested environments. These settings are characterized
by high pedestrian traffic, chaos, irregular road structures,
and relatively narrow pathways. However, we observed a
notable absence of pedestrian trajectory datasets tailored
to these specific scenarios, presenting an opportunity for
in-depth exploration. To address this research gap, this article
introduces a novel dataset, referred to as VPT. Distinguishing
itself from the existing studies, our dataset is collected within
the campus and surrounding environments through digital twin
technology [15], [16], aligning more closely with practical
applications and establishing a foundation for the future appli-
cation of autonomous driving technology in such intricate
settings.
Recent advancements in diffusion modeling methodologies,
particularly in fields such as image generation, have been
noteworthy. In this study, the concept of diffusion modeling is
applied to depict the motion of pedestrians analogous to par-
ticles within nonequilibrium thermodynamics. The diffusion
model is incorporated into the decoder segment of the CV AE
framework. Enhanced by the universal transformer architec-
ture, this model adeptly mitigates uncertainty in pedestrian
motion, resulting in the derivation of deterministic trajecto-
ries. We recognize the inadequacies of traditional methods in
addressing the complexities inherent in campus and surround-
ing scenarios. Our approach mitigates these shortcomings
by introducing diffusion modeling, resulting in a significant
advantage when applied to the new dataset. This adaptation
enhances its suitability for environments characterized by high
pedestrian traffic, disorderliness, and irregularities, making it
particularly well-suited for scenarios with substantial uncer-
tainty and nonuniform conditions. It is worth emphasizing
that our approach is not confined to specific scenarios; rather,
it demonstrates commendable performance even in conven-
tional autonomous driving settings. The main contribution of
this article is as follows.
1) We introduce a learning-based integrated approach,
named universal transformer diffusion modeling for
vulnerable pedestrian trajectory prediction (UTD-PTP),
which combines diffusion modeling of pedestrian tra-
jectory uncertainty based on the universal transformer
framework. UTD-PTP is the first to effectively integrate
the universal transformer with a diffusion model for
trajectory prediction framework.
2) We have curated a dataset for vulnerable pedestrian
trajectory (VPT) prediction using the digital twin
technique.
3) The approach proposed in this article achieves state-of-
the-art performance on the widely adopted pedestrian
trajectory prediction dataset and VPT.
II. R ELATED WORK
A. T rajectory Forecasting Approaches for Pedestrian
Accurate pedestrian trajectory prediction enhances safety,
interactivity, and efficiency in the field of autonomous driving.
Currently, a substantial body of research on pedestrian trajec-
tory prediction exists, primarily divided into two directions:
physics-based prediction and data-driven prediction. Physics-
based modeling techniques, such as SFM [17], effectively
model pedestrian trajectories during the prediction process but
lack the flexibility to adapt to complex scenarios. Data-driven

methods excel in capturing inherent complexity in trajec-
tory interactions, thereby improving prediction accuracy [18].
However, deterministic data-driven methods such as GRU [19]
and MSFM [5] struggle to model the uncertainty of pedes-
trian trajectories. In semi-closed campuses and surrounding
scenarios with narrow roads and complex network structures,
this environment poses greater challenges for deterministic
pedestrian trajectory prediction. Recently, TRACE [20], based
on the diffusion model, has shown outstanding performance
in closed-loop pedestrian trajectory generation. Motivated by
this, this article proposes a novel approach using deep gen-
erative models [21] and the diffusion model to encapsulate
the complex process supporting the modeling of uncertainty
variations in pedestrian trajectory distributions in semi-open
scenarios. This work pioneers the application of the diffusion
model in predicting the trajectories of vulnerable pedestrians
in semi-open scenarios.
B. Denoising Diffusion Models in Pedestrian
T rajectory Prediction
Denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) is a class
of deep generative models that draw inspiration from the
principles of nonequilibrium thermodynamics within the realm
of physics [22]. Conceptually, it simulates the diffusion pro-
cess like that found in nonequilibrium statistical physics;
subsequently, it leverages denoising models to reconstruct data
distributions. Notably, DDPM has demonstrated remarkable
efficacy in various generative tasks, including but not limited
to image synthesis, point cloud generation, and audio synthe-
sis [23]. Furthermore, there have been endeavors to extend
the utility of DDPM to the domain of trajectory prediction.
MID [24] pioneered the utilization of diffusion models to
model temporal variations in predictive uncertainty of trajec-
tory forecasts. Nevertheless, given the considerable temporal
overhead associated with conventional diffusion models, which
involve an extensive number of diffusion steps, the LED [25]
methodology advances the proposal to use denoising diffusion
implicit models (DDIM) [26] as a means to expedite the
sampling process. By doing so, this approach seeks to curtail
temporal expenses and enhance overall computational effi-
ciency. Nonetheless, all the techniques above have primarily
been used for predicting pedestrian trajectories within typical
scenarios and have not been specifically further refined to
address the distinctive requirements associated with semi-open
environments.
C. Pedestrian T rajectory Prediction Dataset in
Semi-Open Environments
The precise anticipation of pedestrian trajectories, par-
ticularly those individuals who are actively interacting
with autonomous vehicles, assumes paramount importance
in enhancing driving safety [27]. The existing corpus of
pedestrian trajectory prediction datasets is predominantly con-
centrated within conventional scenarios, encompassing open
roadways, junctions, and roundabouts [28]. Conversely, there
exists a scarcity of datasets that systematically address the
intricacies of pedestrian trajectory prediction in semi-open
environments. With the advancement of autonomous driving
technology, the demand for autonomous delivery vehicles,
such as automated food delivery and courier services in
semi-open scenarios, is increasing. This study introduces a
novel dataset specifically designed for predicting pedestrian
trajectories in semi-open scenarios (campus and surrounding
residential areas).
III. P ROPOSED METHOD
In this section, we present the UTD-PTP, an innovative
approach for trajectory forecasting that integrates a univer-
sal transformer within a diffusion model to enhance the
accuracy of human trajectory prediction. We first explicitly
formulate the uncertainty variation as an inverse diffusion
process based on DDIMs. We then describe how, compared
with the traditional diffusion models based on the U-net
network [29], this article innovatively introduces the universal
transformer architecture for the proposed UTD-PTP method.
Finally, we describe how to train this diffusion model.

B. Denoising Diffusion Model for Pedestrian
T rajectory Prediction
Diffusion models have succeeded substantially across var-
ious generative tasks [30]. In the context of trajectory
prediction, one notable advantage of using this approach lies
in its capacity to conceptualize the reduction of prediction
uncertainty as an inverse diffusion process [24]. Owing to
the inherent inclusion of a substantial number of diffusion
steps within conventional diffusion models, their application
to predicting multiagent human trajectories for vulnerable
pedestrians necessitates a considerable expenditure of com-
putational resources. Hence, we present the UTD-PTP model
in this study, depicted in the encoder block of Fig. 1. The
diffusion step has been condensed into two steps to mitigate
the computational resource overhead. This section will first
elaborate on how to model the diffusion process and the
inverse diffusion process using the ordinary diffusion model.
It will then explain how to significantly reduce the diffusion
steps based on the ordinary diffusion model through the
principles of DDIM.

C. Universal T ransformer Framework for VPT Prediction
The diffusion model used for human trajectory prediction
exhibits certain efficacy [20]. However, the inclusion of the
implicit diffusion sample module, as adopted in this study to
mitigate data consumption, may inadvertently overlook local
correlations and fine-grained information. In this research,
we introduce a universal transformer framework [31], which,
unlike conventional transformers, offers the improved capabil-
ity to effectively address the precision deficit attributed to the
implicit diffusion sample module in the trajectory generation
process. The universal transformer uses self-attention across
all the input data in a sequence. Self-attention exclusively
relies on the input from the current time step to calculate
correlations with each temporal, thereby deriving the associ-
ated weights. As depicted in the decoder and prediction block
of Fig. 1, this inherent characteristic endows self-attention
with the capability to concurrently reason across multiple time
steps during trajectory prediction, without any dependency on
preceding time steps. At the given time step, denoted as t, the
input feature to the module is represented as Ht, comprising
three distinct tensors: query ( Q), key ( K ), and value ( V ). The
input may be represented in the following manner :
Q = Wq · Ht
K = Wk · Ht
V = Wv · Ht . (17)
Herein, the matrices {Wq , Wk, Wv} denote the convolution
weights used in this formulation. The process of similarity cal-
culation involves the computation of similarity scores between
discrete points within a historical trajectory. This computation
is facilitated by means of the dot product operation, which
serves as a metric for quantifying the overarching similarity
between individual coordinates, specifically in the context of
the x and y dimensions. This computational procedure can be
expressed as follows :

D. T raining Objective
To predict the actual trajectory xk, the goal of the training is
to maximize the variational lower bound of the inverse process,
which is due to the fact that it is not feasible to compute
the exact log-likelihood estimate directly. The formula for the
variational lower bound is given below
L(θ, ψ)= Eq [GKL(q(xk−1 | xk, x0)pθ (xk − 1 | xk, f ))
− log pθ (x0 | x1, f )] (22)
where GKL is the Kullback–Leibler scatter, q(xk−1 | xk, x0)
is the posterior distribution, pθ (xk − 1 | xk, f ) is the con-
ditional distribution of the model, and log pθ (x0 | x1, f ) is
the log-likelihood of the target trajectory x0. The negative
variational lower bound from the above formula is used as
the loss function, denoted as L(θ, ψ). The goal of this part is
to minimize this difference, ensuring accurate trajectory pre-
diction by the model. In the loss function, a parameterization
method is used to reparameterize the model’s conditional
distribution for better training. The training process is executed
at each step k(k ∈ 1, 2, . . . ,k), where the model’s parameters
θ and ψ are updated by maximizing the loss function L(θ, ψ).
This process iterates continuously to enhance the model’s
predictive performance on trajectory data.
IV. VPT P REDICTION DATASET
Autonomous delivery vehicles, particularly those used for
food delivery and package services, have become widespread
in semi-open environments such as university campuses.
However, there is a scarcity of publicly available datasets
specifically tailored for predicting pedestrian trajectories in
such semi-open settings. Most existing pedestrian trajectory
datasets are built upon open road scenarios, contributing to a
limitation in the performance of autonomous vehicles operat-
ing in these semi-open environments. This article introduces
the “VPT Prediction Dataset,” aiming to address the gap
in pedestrian trajectory datasets for autonomous vehicles in
semi-open campus scenarios. In the interest of upholding
pedestrian privacy, the pedestrian trajectory data incorporated
into this dataset have been carefully curated as simulated
pedestrian trajectories [40]. The vector map derived from this
dataset is constructed using digital twin [41], which replicates
a physical environment with a latitude span of 0.0302 and a
longitude span of 0.0428, using the OSM file sourced from
OpenStreetMap. This is achieved by importing the actual
road network structure from the OSM file into SUMO and
converting it into a net.xml file. Subsequently, by integrat-
ing this genuine road network structure with the rou.xml
flow file, pedestrian trajectory information is extracted. These
trajectories are generated in alignment with the authentic
road environments surrounding six prominent academic insti-
tutions located in Beijing, China, as shown in Fig. 2. The
dataset, employing identical evaluation metrics as delineated in
Table I, yielded experimental outcomes delineated in Table II.
The comprehensive data volume and distribution of roads are
delineated in Table III.
The evaluation of this dataset’s benchmark was conducted
using established standard metrics. Consistent with the evalu-
ation method used in Argoverse 2 [42], we used the following
metrics: ADE, which calculates the average L2 distance
between the best-predicted trajectory and the ground-truth
trajectory, and FDE, which measures the L2 distance between
the best predicted trajectory and the ground-truth trajectory.

emergency braking by vehicles 0.16 s in advance can lead

Therefore, the investigation of pedestrian trajectory predic-
tion is essential within the domain of autonomous driving,
as it holds the promise of elevating safety and optimizing

The task of predicting pedestrian trajectories involves fore-
casting the future trajectory coordinates of a known historical
trajectory. Previous studies initially tackled the pedestrian
trajectory prediction task using kinematic models, specifi-
cally

including the constant velocity (CV) model [6] and
constant acceleration (CA) model [7]. Models of this kind
use fundamental models such as recursive Bayesian filters
to formulate pedestrian states, demonstrating relatively good
performance and low computational costs. Recently, the con-
cept of stochastic trajectory prediction has been introduced
to capture the possibilities of future pedestrian trajectories,
aiming to accurately model the distribution of future trajec-
tories. Currently, methods based on deep learning generative
models have achieved significant success in this field. Some
approaches leverage generative adversarial networks (GANs)
[8] to model the future distribution of trajectories, while others
integrate conditional variational autoencoders (CV AEs) [9] to
provide more stable model training solutions, thereby better
adapting to the target distribution.
While current research has made notable progress in
addressing the challenge of predicting pedestrian trajectories,
there exists a comparative limitation. The dynamic evolution
of uncertainty in pedestrian trajectories is not adequately
modeled. The trajectory motion of pedestrians is characterized
by a notable degree of uncertainty, primarily stemming from
the inherent unpredictability of human behavior [10]. Explicit
representation is typically based on physical principles and
kinematic laws [11], which can more accurately represent
the interaction between agents [6]. However, due to the
limited depth of this representation, the prediction model
is not comprehensive [12]. Implicit representations are not
directly observable and require inference or learning. These
features may encompass the pedestrian’s intentions, behavioral
patterns, habitual movements, and so forth [13]. Implicit
features offer a

more comprehensive context and compre-
hension, thereby enhancing the accuracy and robustness of
trajectory prediction [14]. In contrast to prior methodologies
using explicit representations for extracting trajectory features,
the objective of this article is to leverage implicit repre-
sentations for modeling the diffusion of motion uncertainty,
thereby shaping stochastic trajectory predictions. In addition,
in the domain of pedestrian trajectory prediction, construct-
ing effective datasets and proposing corresponding solutions
represent the primary steps in addressing challenges. Ini-
tially, we investigated the application of pedestrian trajectory
prediction methods in campus and surrounding scenarios.
The continuous evolution of autonomous driving technology
has introduced new demands, such as the navigation of
autonomous delivery vehicles and courier vehicles in intricate
and congested environments. These settings are characterized
by high pedestrian traffic, chaos, irregular road structures,
and relatively narrow pathways. However, we observed a
notable absence of pedestrian trajectory datasets tailored
to these specific scenarios, presenting an opportunity for
in-depth exploration. To address this research gap, this article
introduces a novel dataset, referred to as VPT. Distinguishing
itself from the existing studies, our dataset is collected within
the campus and surrounding environments through digital twin
technology [15], [16], aligning more closely with practical
applications and establishing a foundation for the future appli-
cation of autonomous driving technology in such intricate
settings.
Recent advancements in diffusion modeling methodologies,
particularly in fields such as image generation, have been
noteworthy. In this study, the concept of diffus

ion modeling is
applied to depict the motion of pedestrians analogous to par-
ticles within nonequilibrium thermodynamics. The diffusion
model is incorporated into the decoder segment of the CV AE
framework. Enhanced by the universal transformer architec-
ture, this model adeptly mitigates uncertainty in pedestrian
motion, resulting in the derivation of deterministic trajecto-
ries. We recognize the inadequacies of traditional methods in
addressing the complexities inherent in campus and surround-
ing scenarios. Our approach mitigates these shortcomings
by introducing diffusion modeling, resulting in a significant
advantage when applied to the new dataset. This adaptation
enhances its suitability for environments characterized by high
pedestrian traffic, disorderliness, and irregularities, making it
particularly well-suited for scenarios with substantial uncer-
tainty and nonuniform conditions. It is worth emphasizing
that our approach is not confined to specific scenarios; rather,
it demonstrates commendable performance even in conven-
tional autonomous driving settings. The main contribution of
this article is as follows.
1) We introduce a learning-based integrated approach,
named universal transformer diffusion modeling for
vulnerable pedestrian trajectory prediction (UTD-PTP),
which combines diffusion modeling of pedestrian tra-
jectory uncertainty based on the universal transformer
framework. UTD-PTP is the first to effectively integrate
the universal transformer with a diffusion model for
trajectory prediction framework.
2) We have curated a dataset for vulnerable pedestrian
trajectory (VPT) prediction using the digital twin
technique.
3) The approach proposed in this article achieves state-of-
the-art performance on the widely adopted pedestrian
trajectory predic

tion dataset and VPT.
II. R ELATED WORK
A. T rajectory Forecasting Approaches for Pedestrian
Accurate pedestrian trajectory prediction enhances safety,
interactivity, and efficiency in the field of autonomous driving.
Currently, a substantial body of research on pedestrian trajec-
tory prediction exists, primarily divided into two directions:
physics-based prediction and data-driven prediction. Physics-
based modeling techniques, such as SFM [17], effectively
model pedestrian trajectories during the prediction process but
lack the flexibility to adapt to complex scenarios. Data-driven

methods excel in capturing inherent complexity in trajec-
tory interactions, thereby improving prediction accuracy [18].
However, deterministic data-driven methods such as GRU [19]
and MSFM [5] struggle to model the uncertainty of pedes-
trian trajectories. In semi-closed campuses and surrounding
scenarios with narrow roads and complex network structures,
this environment poses greater challenges for deterministic
pedestrian trajectory prediction. Recently, TRACE [20], based
on the diffusion model, has shown outstanding performance
in closed-loop pedestrian trajectory generation. Motivated by
this, this article proposes a novel approach using deep gen-
erative models [21] and the diffusion model to encapsulate
the complex process supporting the modeling of uncertainty
variations in pedestrian trajectory distributions in semi-open
scenarios. This work pioneers the application of the diffusion
model in predicting the trajectories of vulnerable pedestrians
in semi-open scenari

os.
B. Denoising Diffusion Models in Pedestrian
T rajectory Prediction
Denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) is a class
of deep generative models that draw inspiration from the
principles of nonequilibrium thermodynamics within the realm
of physics [22]. Conceptually, it simulates the diffusion pro-
cess like that found in nonequilibrium statistical physics;
subsequently, it leverages denoising models to reconstruct data
distributions. Notably, DDPM has demonstrated remarkable
efficacy in various generative tasks, including but not limited
to image synthesis, point cloud generation, and audio synthe-
sis [23]. Furthermore, there have been endeavors to extend
the utility of DDPM to the domain of trajectory prediction.
MID [24] pioneered the utilization of diffusion models to
model temporal variations in predictive uncertainty of trajec-
tory forecasts. Nevertheless, given the considerable temporal
overhead associated with conventional diffusion models, which
involve an extensive number of diffusion steps, the LED [25]
methodology advances the proposal to use denoising diffusion
implicit models (DDIM) [26] as a means to expedite the
sampling process. By doing so, this approach seeks to curtail
temporal expenses and enhance overall computational effi-
ciency. Nonetheless, all the techniques above have primarily
been used for predicting pedestrian trajectories within typical
scenarios and have not been specifically further refined to
address the distinctive requirements associated with semi-open
environments.
C. Pedestrian T rajectory Prediction Dataset in
Semi-Open Environments
The precise anticipation of pedestrian trajectories, par-
ticularly those individuals who are actively interacting
with autonomous vehicles, assumes paramount importance
in enhancing drivin

g safety [27]. The existing corpus of
pedestrian trajectory prediction datasets is predominantly con-
centrated within conventional scenarios, encompassing open
roadways, junctions, and roundabouts [28]. Conversely, there
exists a scarcity of datasets that systematically address the
intricacies of pedestrian trajectory prediction in semi-open
environments. With the advancement of autonomous driving
technology, the demand for autonomous delivery vehicles,
such as automated food delivery and courier services in
semi-open scenarios, is increasing. This study introduces a
novel dataset specifically designed for predicting pedestrian
trajectories in semi-open scenarios (campus and surrounding
residential areas).
III. P ROPOSED METHOD
In this section, we present the UTD-PTP, an innovative
approach for trajectory forecasting that integrates a univer-
sal transformer within a diffusion model to enhance the
accuracy of human trajectory prediction. We first explicitly
formulate the uncertainty variation as an inverse diffusion
process based on DDIMs. We then describe how, compared
with the traditional diffusion models based on the U-net
network [29], this article innovatively introduces the universal
transformer architecture for the proposed UTD-PTP method.
Finally, we describe how to train this diffusion model.

B. Denoising Diffusion Model for Pedestrian

Diffusion models have succeeded substantially across var-

prediction, one notable advantage of using this approach lies
in its capacity to conceptualize the reduction of prediction
uncertainty as an inverse diffusion process [24]. Owing to
the inherent inclusion of a substantial number of diffusion
steps within conventional diffusion models, their application
to predicting multiagent human trajectories for vulnerable
pedestrians necessitates a considerable expenditure of com-
putational resources. Hence, we present the UTD-PTP model
in this study, depicted in the encoder block of Fig. 1. The
diffusion step has been condensed into two steps to mitigate
the computational resource overhead. This section will first
elaborate on how to model the diffusion process and the
inverse diffusion process using the ordinary diffusion model.
It will then explain how to significantly reduce the diffusion
steps based on the ordinary diffusion model through the
principles of DDIM.

C. Universal T ransformer Framework for VPT Prediction
The diffusion model used for human trajectory prediction
exhibits certain efficacy [20]. However, the inclusion of the
implicit diffusion sample module, as adopted in this study to
mitigate data consumption, may inadvertently overlook local
correlations and fine-grained information. In this research,
we introduce a universal transformer framework [31], which,
unlike conventional transformers, offers the improved capabil-
ity to effectively address the precision deficit attributed to the
implicit diffusion sample module in the trajectory generation
process. The universal transformer uses self-attention across
all the input data in a sequence. Self-attention exclusively
relies on the input from the current time step to calculate
correlations with each temporal, thereby deriving the associ-
ated weights. As depicted in the decoder and prediction block
of Fig. 1, this inherent characteristic endows self-attention
with the capability to concurrently reason across multiple time
steps during trajectory prediction, without any dependency on
preceding time steps. At the given time step, denoted as t, the
input feature to the module is represented as Ht, comprising
three distinct tensors: query ( Q), key ( K ), and value ( V ). The
input may be represented in the following manner :
Q = Wq · Ht
K = Wk · Ht
V = Wv · Ht . (17)
Herein, the matrices {Wq , Wk, Wv} denote the convolution
weights used in this formulation. The process of similarity cal-
culation involves the computat

discrete points within a historical trajectory. This computation
is facilitated by means of the dot product operation, which
serves as a metric for quantifying the overarching similarity
between individual coordinates, specifically in the context of
the x and y dimensions. This computational procedure can be
expressed as follows :

D. T raining Objective
To predict the actual trajectory xk, the goal of the training is
to maximize the variational lower bound of the inverse process,
which is due to the fact that it is not feasible to compute
the exact log-likelihood estimate directly. The formula for the
variational lower bound is given below
L(θ, ψ)= Eq [GKL(q(xk−1 | xk, x0)pθ (xk − 1 | xk, f ))
− log pθ (x0 | x1, f )] (22)
where GKL is the Kullback–Leibler scatter, q(xk−1 | xk, x0)
is the posterior distribution, pθ (xk − 1 | xk, f ) is the con-
ditional distribution of the model, and log pθ (x0 | x1, f ) is
the log-likelihood of the target trajectory x0. The negative
variational lower bound from the above formula is used as
the loss function, denoted as L(θ, ψ). The goal of this part is
to minimize this difference, ensuring accurate trajectory pre-
diction by the model. In the loss function, a parameterization
method is used to reparameterize the model’s conditional
distribution for better training. The training process is executed
at each step k(k ∈ 1, 2, . . . ,k), where the model’s parameters
θ and ψ are updated by maximizing the loss function L(θ, ψ).
This process iterates continuously to enhance the model’s
pr

edictive performance on trajectory data.
IV. VPT P REDICTION DATASET
Autonomous delivery vehicles, particularly those used for
food delivery and package services, have become widespread
in semi-open environments such as university campuses.
However, there is a scarcity of publicly available datasets
specifically tailored for predicting pedestrian trajectories in
such semi-open settings. Most existing pedestrian trajectory
datasets are built upon open road scenarios, contributing to a
limitation in the performance of autonomous vehicles operat-
ing in these semi-open environments. This article introduces
the “VPT Prediction Dataset,” aiming to address the gap
in pedestrian trajectory datasets for autonomous vehicles in
semi-open campus scenarios. In the interest of upholding
pedestrian privacy, the pedestrian trajectory data incorporated
into this dataset have been carefully curated as simulated
pedestrian trajectories [40]. The vector map derived from this
dataset is constructed using digital twin [41], which replicates
a physical environment with a latitude span of 0.0302 and a
longitude span of 0.0428, using the OSM file sourced from
OpenStreetMap. This is achieved by importing the actual
road network structure from the OSM file into SUMO and
converting it into a net.xml file. Subsequently, by integrat-
ing this genuine road network structure with the rou.xml
flow file, pedestrian trajectory information is extracted. These
trajectories are generated in alignment with the authentic
road environments surrounding six prominent academic insti-
tutions located in Beijing, China, as shown in Fig. 2. The
dataset, employing identical evaluation metrics as delineated in
Table I, yielded experimental outcomes delineated in Table II.
The comprehensive data volume and distributio

n of roads are
delineated in Table III.
The evaluation of this dataset’s benchmark was conducted
using established standard metrics. Consistent with the evalu-
ation method used in Argoverse 2 [42], we used the following
metrics: ADE, which calculates the average L2 distance
between the best-predicted trajectory and the ground-truth
trajectory, and FDE, which measures the L2 distance between
the best predicted trajectory and the ground-truth trajectory.

V. E XPERIMENTS
A. Experimental Setup
In this section, we conduct experiments on the ETH/UCY
[43], [44] and VPT datasets. A uniform experimental config-
uration is applied to both the datasets, comprising 90 training
epochs with a learning rate of 0.001 and an encoder dimension
of 256. Furthermore, the optimization process is facilitated
through the utilization of the adam optimizer, with a dropout
rate set at 0.5. All the models are implemented with PyTorch
1.7.1 on GTX-4090 GPU.

the proposed method, UTD-PTP, and several state-of-the-art
models on the ETH/UCY
